quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 1, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Limiting Reactants
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Limiting Reactant Calculations, Stoichiometry, Mole Ratios, Identifying Excess Reactants

questions.question: 2 moles of H₂ react with 1 mole of O₂. If 3 moles of H₂ and 2 moles of O₂ are mixed, the limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O
Mole ratio H₂:O₂ = 2:1
For 2 moles O₂, 4 moles H₂ are required. Only 3 moles H₂ are available, so H₂ is insufficient.
For 3 moles H₂, 1.5 moles O₂ are required. 2 moles O₂ are available, so O₂ is in excess.
Therefore, H₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: When 5 g of Na reacts with 10 g of Cl₂, the limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl
Molar mass Na = 23 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Na = 5/23 = 0.217 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 10/71 = 0.141 mol
Mole ratio Na:Cl₂ = 2:1
For 0.141 mol Cl₂, 0.282 mol Na are required. Only 0.217 mol Na are available.
Therefore, Na is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: In the reaction N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃, 2 moles of N₂ react with 5 moles of H₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio N₂:H₂ = 1:3
For 2 moles N₂, 6 moles H₂ are required. Only 5 moles H₂ are available.
Therefore, H₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: 10 g of Al reacts with 30 g of O₂ to form Al₂O₃. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: 4Al + 3O₂ → 2Al₂O₃
Molar mass Al = 27 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles Al = 10/27 = 0.370 mol
Moles O₂ = 30/32 = 0.938 mol
Mole ratio Al:O₂ = 4:3
For 0.370 mol Al, (3/4) × 0.370 = 0.278 mol O₂ are required. 0.938 mol O₂ are available, so O₂ is in excess.
Therefore, Al is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: When 50 g of Fe reacts with 32 g of O₂, forming Fe₂O₃, the limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃
Molar mass Fe = 56 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles Fe = 50/56 = 0.893 mol
Moles O₂ = 32/32 = 1.0 mol
Mole ratio Fe:O₂ = 4:3
For 1.0 mol O₂, (4/3) × 1.0 = 1.33 mol Fe are required. Only 0.893 mol Fe are available.
Therefore, Fe is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: C + O₂ → CO₂. If 12 g of C is mixed with 32 g of O₂, the limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass C = 12 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles C = 12/12 = 1 mol
Moles O₂ = 32/32 = 1 mol
The reaction is 1:1, so both reactants are present in exact stoichiometric amounts.
Neither reactant is limiting; both are completely consumed.

questions.question: 2 moles of K react with 1 mole of Br₂ to form KBr. If 4 moles of K and 3 moles of Br₂ are mixed, the limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: 2K + Br₂ → 2KBr
Mole ratio K:Br₂ = 2:1
For 3 moles Br₂, 6 moles K are required. Only 4 moles K are available.
Therefore, K is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: H₂ + Cl₂ → 2HCl. If 2 moles of H₂ react with 1 mole of Cl₂, the limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
The reaction is 1:1.
For 2 moles H₂, 2 moles Cl₂ are required. Only 1 mole Cl₂ is available.
Therefore, Cl₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: In the reaction 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl, 5 g of Na reacts with 8 g of Cl₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Na = 23 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Na = 5/23 = 0.217 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 8/71 = 0.113 mol
Mole ratio Na:Cl₂ = 2:1
For 0.113 mol Cl₂, 0.226 mol Na are required. Only 0.217 mol Na are available.
Therefore, Na is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: In the reaction 4Al + 3O₂ → 2Al₂O₃, 16 g of Al reacts with 48 g of O₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Al = 27 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles Al = 16/27 = 0.593 mol
Moles O₂ = 48/32 = 1.5 mol
Mole ratio Al:O₂ = 4:3
For 0.593 mol Al, (3/4) × 0.593 = 0.445 mol O₂ are required. 1.5 mol O₂ are available, so O₂ is in excess.
Therefore, Al is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. If 3 moles of H₂ react with 2 moles of O₂, the limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio H₂:O₂ = 2:1
For 2 moles O₂, 4 moles H₂ are required. Only 3 moles H₂ are available.
Therefore, H₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: C₃H₈ + 5O₂ → 3CO₂ + 4H₂O. 3 moles of C₃H₈ react with 10 moles of O₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio C₃H₈:O₂ = 1:5
For 3 moles C₃H₈, 15 moles O₂ are required. Only 10 moles O₂ are available.
Therefore, O₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂. 6 g of Zn reacts with 12 g of HCl. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Zn = 65 g/mol, HCl = 36.5 g/mol
Moles Zn = 6/65 = 0.0923 mol
Moles HCl = 12/36.5 = 0.329 mol
Mole ratio Zn:HCl = 1:2
For 0.329 mol HCl, 0.165 mol Zn are required. Only 0.0923 mol Zn are available.
Therefore, Zn is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: 2K + Cl₂ → 2KCl. 5 moles of K and 2 moles of Cl₂ react. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio K:Cl₂ = 2:1
For 5 moles K, 2.5 moles Cl₂ are required. Only 2 moles Cl₂ are available.
Therefore, Cl₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: Fe + S → FeS. If 56 g of Fe reacts with 32 g of S, the limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Fe = 56 g/mol, S = 32 g/mol
Moles Fe = 56/56 = 1 mol
Moles S = 32/32 = 1 mol
The reaction is 1:1, so both reactants are present in exact stoichiometric amounts.
Neither reactant is limiting; both are completely consumed.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered limiting reactant calculations! 🧪⚖️
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review mole ratios and how to identify the limiting reactant.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on comparing required moles with available moles.
scoreMessages.0: Please review stoichiometry, mole ratios, and limiting reactant concepts.
